categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Rulers, dynasties & the consolidation of Christian empire

entries.label: Diocletian (abdication)
entries.hint: Diocletian abdicates voluntarily on 1 May 305 — the only emperor in this era to retire peacefully; his palace at Split still inhabited today.
entries.note: Abdication date: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 19. Palace at Split (Spalatum): extant, UNESCO. Lactantius and Eutropius 9.26 both record the abdication.

entries.label: Galerius
entries.hint: Eastern Augustus after Diocletian's abdication; issued the Edict of Toleration (311) on his deathbed, ending the Great Persecution.
entries.note: Edict of Toleration 311: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 33–34 (full text). Eusebius HE 8.17. Died May 311 — both sources agree.

entries.label: Maxentius
entries.hint: Son of Maximian who seized power in Rome against the Tetrarchic system; controlled Italy and Africa until defeated and drowned at the Milvian Bridge.
entries.note: SHA Vita Maxentii (disputed authenticity). Defeated at Milvian Bridge 28 Oct 312 — Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 44; Eusebius Vita Const. 1.37–38.

entries.label: Constantine I ('the Great')
entries.hint: Reunified the empire, legalised Christianity, founded Constantinople, convened Nicaea — the most consequential emperor since Augustus.
entries.note: Victory at Milvian Bridge 28 Oct 312: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 44; Eusebius VC 1.28–29. Death 22 May 337 at Nicomedia — Eusebius VC 4.62. Extensive coinage throughout reign.

entries.label: Licinius
entries.hint: Constantine's co-emperor and brother-in-law who co-signed the Edict of Milan (313); defeated and executed by Constantine in 324.
entries.note: Edict of Milan 313: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 48 (full Latin text). Defeated at Chrysopolis 18 Sep 324 — Zosimus 2.25; Eusebius VC 2.15–18.

entries.label: Constantius II
entries.hint: Sole emperor from 353; devoted Arian Christian who expelled the pro-Nicene bishop Athanasius five times and tried to impose Arian Christianity empire-wide.
entries.note: Athanasius's own letters document the five exiles. Ammianus Marcellinus 21.16 records Constantius's death 3 Nov 361 at Mopsucrene. Extensive Arian synod records survive.

entries.label: Julian ('the Apostate')
entries.hint: Constantine's nephew who publicly renounced Christianity and attempted to restore traditional Roman religion; died on the Persian campaign he himself launched.
entries.note: Julian's own writings survive: Orations, Letters, Against the Galileans (fragments). Died 26 Jun 363 near Samarra — Ammianus Marcellinus 25.3. Libanius Oratio 18 (funeral oration).

entries.label: Jovian
entries.hint: Christian general elected by the army on Julian's death; concluded the humiliating peace with Persia ceding Nisibis, then died after 8 months.
entries.note: Peace of 363: Ammianus Marcellinus 25.7 records terms. Death at Dadastana: Ammianus 25.10. Eutropius 10.17 confirms date. Nisibis cession documented in multiple sources.

entries.label: Valentinian I (West)
entries.hint: Capable military emperor who spent his reign fortifying the Rhine frontier; died of a stroke while angrily haranguing a Gothic delegation.
entries.note: Death 17 Nov 375 at Brigetio — Ammianus Marcellinus 30.6. Rhine fortification programme: Ammianus 28.2. Zosimus 4.17. Multiple inscription evidence from Rhine forts.

entries.label: Valens (East)
entries.hint: Eastern emperor who admitted the Goths across the Danube in 376 and was killed at the Battle of Adrianople — his body never found.
entries.note: Death at Adrianople 9 Aug 378 — Ammianus Marcellinus 31.13. Zosimus 4.24. Eunapius fragments. Body never recovered: Ammianus 31.13.15.

entries.label: Gratian (West)
entries.hint: Young Western emperor who removed the Altar of Victory from the Senate and stripped pagan priests of their stipends; murdered in a military revolt.
entries.note: Altar of Victory dispute: Symmachus Relatio 3 (384) and Ambrose Epistulae 17–18 (both survive in full). Murdered Lugdunum 25 Aug 383 — Zosimus 4.35.

entries.label: Theodosius I (East, then sole)
entries.hint: Made Christianity the only legal religion of the empire (380 & 391); baptised on his deathbed in 380, then became sole emperor — the last to rule a united empire.
entries.note: Edict of Thessalonica 27 Feb 380: Codex Theodosianus 16.1.2 (full text survives). Sole emperor from 394. Death 17 Jan 395 at Milan — Zosimus 4.59; Claudian's panegyric.

entries.label: Battle of the Milvian Bridge
entries.loc: Pons Milvius, north of Rome (mod. Ponte Milvio)
entries.hint: Constantine defeats Maxentius, who drowns in the Tiber — the battle after which Constantine attributes his victory to the Christian god.
entries.note: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 44 (chi-rho dream). Eusebius VC 1.28–29 (cross in sky). Panegyrici Latini 12 (313) — contemporary oration. Bridge and site identified.

entries.label: Battle of Chrysopolis
entries.hint: Constantine defeats Licinius to become sole emperor of the reunified Roman world — the last civil war until the 350s.
entries.note: Zosimus 2.25. Eusebius VC 2.15–18. Anonymus Valesianus 5.27–28. Licinius subsequently executed — Zosimus 2.28.

entries.label: Julian's Persian campaign
entries.hint: Julian leads a massive invasion of Persia, reaches Ctesiphon but cannot take it, then is mortally wounded during the retreat — ending the campaign in disaster.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus 23–25 (detailed eyewitness account). Julian's own letters document preparations. Death 26 Jun 363: Ammianus 25.3. Zosimus 3.12–29.

entries.label: Peace of 363 — cession of Nisibis
entries.loc: Nisibis (mod. Nusaybin, Turkey) ceded to Persia
entries.hint: Rome cedes Nisibis and 15 frontier towns to Persia — the most humiliating territorial concession in Roman history, forced after Julian's disastrous campaign.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus 25.7 records full terms. Ephrem the Syrian's Carmina Nisibena (Syriac poems) document the Roman evacuation — primary eyewitness.

entries.label: Gothic crossing of the Danube
entries.hint: Valens admits around 200,000 Goths fleeing the Huns across the Danube — the largest single migration into the empire, and the event that leads directly to Adrianople.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus 31.3–4. Eunapius fragments. Scale and circumstances: Heather, Goths and Romans (1991). Papyrus evidence for administrative processing of refugees.

entries.label: Battle of Adrianople
entries.hint: The Roman army is annihilated by the Goths; emperor Valens killed — the worst Roman defeat since Cannae and a turning point in late Roman history.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus 31.12–13 (detailed, near-contemporary). Eunapius. Orosius 7.33. Valens's body never found: Ammianus 31.13.15. Date confirmed by multiple sources.

entries.label: Battle of the Frigidus
entries.hint: Theodosius defeats the pagan usurper Eugenius in a battle later framed as Christianity defeating paganism — a fierce two-day fight decided partly by a windstorm.
entries.note: Claudian, De III Consulatu Honorii 96–98. Ambrose, De obitu Theodosii 10. Orosius 7.35. The bora windstorm (literary topos or real): Rufinus HE 11.33 records it.

entries.label: Battle of Argentoratum
entries.hint: Julian (then Caesar under Constantius) defeats the Alamanni king Chnodomar with a much smaller Roman force — his greatest military victory.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus 16.12 (detailed). Libanius Oratio 18.56–69. Chnodomar captured and sent to Constantius: Ammianus 16.12.66. Site identified near Strasbourg.

entries.label: Edict of Toleration (Galerius)
entries.hint: Galerius's deathbed edict ending the Great Persecution and explicitly acknowledging that the campaign to suppress Christianity had failed.
entries.note: Full Latin text: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 33–34. Greek version: Eusebius HE 8.17. Dated 30 Apr 311 by consular dating in the text itself.

entries.label: Edict of Milan
entries.hint: Constantine and Licinius agree to full religious toleration and restoration of confiscated Church property — the foundational document of Christian freedom in the West.
entries.note: Full Latin text: Lactantius De Mort. Pers. 48. Greek version: Eusebius HE 10.5. Neither is an 'edict' strictly — a rescript posted at Nicomedia Jun 313.

entries.label: Foundation of Constantinople
entries.hint: Constantine formally dedicates his new eastern capital on 11 May 330 — a city built from scratch to be the Christian capital of a Christian empire.
entries.note: Dedication date: Chronicon Paschale s.a. 330. Zosimus 2.30. Jerome Chronicle s.a. 330. Foundation inscription: partially preserved. Extensive numismatic commemoration.

entries.label: Constantine's Sunday Law
entries.hint: First law mandating rest on Sunday (dies Solis) — bridges pagan solar cult and Christian Sabbath observance in deliberate ambiguity.
entries.note: Codex Justinianus 3.12.2 (full text). Dated 7 Mar 321 by consular dating. Rordorf, Sunday (1968). The law uses 'dies Solis' (Sun's day), not 'Lord's day'.

entries.label: Edict of Thessalonica
entries.hint: Theodosius makes Nicene Christianity the only legal religion of the empire — all other forms of worship become heresy punishable by law.
entries.note: Codex Theodosianus 16.1.2 (full text survives). Dated by internal consular formula to 27 Feb 380. McLynn, Ambrose of Milan (1994) for context.

entries.label: Theodosian edicts banning pagan cult
entries.hint: Theodosius issues a series of edicts closing temples, banning sacrifice and forbidding even entering a temple — effectively outlawing paganism.
entries.note: CTh 16.10.10 (24 Feb 391) and 16.10.12 (8 Nov 392) — both texts survive in Codex Theodosianus. Zosimus 4.36–37 records the effects with hostility.

entries.label: Diocletian's army reforms (legacy)
entries.hint: Diocletian's doubling of the army to ~600,000 and introduction of the comitatenses (mobile reserves) vs. limitanei (frontier troops) shapes 4th-century warfare.
entries.note: John the Lydian, De Mensibus 1.27 records size. Zosimus 2.34 (hostile account). Jones, LRE ch. 17 synthesises papyrus and legal evidence for army structure.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Christianity's rise, councils & the last pagans

entries.label: Council of Nicaea I
entries.hint: First ecumenical council, called by Constantine; defines Christ as 'of one substance with the Father' (homoousios) and sets the date of Easter — foundational for all subsequent Christianity.
entries.note: Acts survive only partially; Eusebius VC 3.6–14 and Athanasius's letters are primary sources. Creed text: Socrates HE 1.8. Date confirmed by multiple chronicles. ~300 bishops attend.

entries.label: Council of Constantinople I
entries.hint: Confirms Nicene Christianity, extends the creed to include the Holy Spirit, and declares Constantinople second in honour after Rome — the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed still used today.
entries.note: Acts survive fragmentarily; Gregory of Nazianzus letter 130 is near-contemporary. Creed text in Theoderet HE 5.9. Socrates HE 5.8. 150 bishops; only Eastern bishops invited.

entries.label: Athanasius vs. Arianism (five exiles)
entries.loc: Alexandria; exile in Trier, Rome, desert
entries.hint: Athanasius is exiled five times by pro-Arian emperors but never recants — his persistence single-handedly keeps Nicene Christianity alive during Constantius II's reign.
entries.note: Athanasius's own Apologia contra Arianos (c.357), Historia Arianorum (c.358), and letters document each exile. Exile dates reconstructed by Barnes (1993). 'Athanasius against the world' (Ath. contra mundum) becomes a byword.

entries.label: Julian's pagan restoration
entries.hint: Julian publicly sacrifices to the old gods, reopens temples, strips the Church of tax privileges and bans Christians from teaching the classics — reversed immediately on his death.
entries.note: Julian: Orations, Letters, Misopogon (Against the Antiochenes, 362) — all survive. CTh 13.3.5 (Jun 362): bans Christian teachers. Ammianus 22.5 describes sacrifices. Gregory of Nazianzus Orationes 4–5 (hostile).

entries.label: Altar of Victory controversy
entries.hint: Gratian removes the Altar of Victory from the Senate; Symmachus writes a famous plea for religious tolerance; Ambrose writes the reply forbidding the Christian emperor to comply.
entries.note: Symmachus Relatio 3 (384): complete text survives. Ambrose Epistulae 17 and 18 (his reply): both survive. Klein (1972). One of the sharpest documented pagan-Christian confrontations.

entries.label: Arian controversy: Council of Sirmium
entries.hint: The 'Blasphemy of Sirmium' — Constantius II's council tries to resolve the Arian controversy by forbidding use of 'substance' language entirely, causing uproar among both sides.
entries.note: Text of the Sirmium formula: Athanasius De Synodis 28 (hostile but verbatim). Hilary of Poitiers De Synodis 11 (same formula with commentary). Jerome: 'the whole world groaned and found itself Arian' — Dial. adv. Lucif. 19.

entries.label: Ulfilas's Gothic Bible translation
entries.loc: Gothic territory north of Danube; later Nicopolis (Bulgaria)
entries.hint: Ulfilas invents the Gothic alphabet and translates the Bible into Gothic — the earliest substantial text in any Germanic language and the vehicle for Arian Christianity among the Goths.
entries.note: Codex Argenteus (Uppsala University Library, DG 1): 188 surviving leaves on purple vellum, 4th–5th c. The only substantial Gothic manuscript. Ulfilas's life: Philostorgius HE 2.5.

entries.label: Arch of Constantine, Rome
entries.hint: Triumphal arch dedicated to Constantine after the Milvian Bridge victory; combines spolia from Trajan, Hadrian and Marcus Aurelius with new Constantinian reliefs — a snapshot of artistic transition.
entries.note: Extant. Dedicatory inscription (CIL VI.1139): 'instinctu divinitatis' — deliberately ambiguous on Christianity. Completed 315 for Constantine's decennalia. L'Orange & von Gerkan (1939).

entries.label: Old St Peter's Basilica, Rome
entries.hint: Constantine's vast basilica over Peter's tomb — the prototype Christian church building; demolished for the present St Peter's but its foundations survive below.
entries.note: Liber Pontificalis records construction under Constantine. Foundations excavated 1940–1950 (Vatican Necropolis). Krautheimer, Corpus Basilicarum Christianarum Romae (1937–77).

entries.label: Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Jerusalem
entries.hint: Constantine builds over the site of Christ's tomb — the most sacred building in Christianity, dedicated 13 Sep 335, still standing though heavily altered.
entries.note: Dedication 13 Sep 335: Eusebius VC 3.25–40 (describes construction). Egeria (Peregrinatio, c.381–384) describes the completed building — earliest pilgrim account. Biddle (1999) on archaeology.

entries.label: Colossal head of Constantine, Rome
entries.loc: Basilica of Maxentius, Rome · Now: Capitoline Museums
entries.hint: Fragment of a 12-metre seated statue of Constantine from the Basilica of Maxentius — the blank, superhuman gaze epitomises the new imperial image.
entries.note: Capitoline Museums, Rome (Inv. 1072). Marble head 2.6m tall. Part of a seated acrolith (marble head/hands, bronze body). Kleiner (1992), Roman Sculpture.

entries.label: Hinton St Mary mosaic (Chi-Rho)
entries.loc: Found: Hinton St Mary, Dorset, England · Now: British Museum
entries.hint: The earliest known image of Christ from Britain — a mosaic floor with a Chi-Rho monogram behind a portrait figure, found in a Roman villa in Dorset.
entries.note: British Museum (1965,0702.1). Found 1963. Dated c.350 by coin evidence. Toynbee (1964), Art in Roman Britain. The identification as Christ (not Constantine) is generally accepted.

entries.label: Esquiline Treasure (partly)
entries.loc: Found: Esquiline Hill, Rome · Now: British Museum / Paris
entries.hint: Hoard of silver plate including the Projecta Casket — aristocratic Christian luxury at its finest, bearing both pagan myth and Christian inscription on the same objects.
entries.note: British Museum, Bibliothèque nationale Paris. Mango (1994), The Sevso Treasure. Kathleen Shelton, The Esquiline Treasure (1981). Projecta Casket: BM 1866,0520.1.

entries.label: Palace of Constantinople (Hippodrome complex)
entries.hint: Constantine transforms Byzantium into a vast imperial capital: hippodrome, imperial palace and Christian basilicas built simultaneously on a massive scale.
entries.note: Hippodrome obelisk inscriptions (extant). Malalas 13.7–8. Eusebius VC 3.48–49. Columns of Serpent (from Delphi), Theodosius, and Constantine still visible in Istanbul.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Intellectual history, theology & the last pagans

entries.label: Eusebius of Caesarea: Ecclesiastical History
entries.hint: The first history of the Christian Church, tracing it from the apostles to Constantine's victory — the founding text of Church historiography, still our primary source for early Christianity.
entries.note: Multiple editions; final edition c.313. Earliest MS: Codex Sinaiticus (4th c.) contains some portions. Schwartz-Mommsen critical edition (1903–09). Lake translation (1926).

entries.label: Athanasius: De Incarnatione
entries.hint: Athanasius's foundational theological argument for why God became man — written before the Arian controversy erupted, it becomes the pro-Nicene position's core text.
entries.note: Complete text survives. Earliest MS: multiple 9th-c. copies. Thomson critical edition (1971). C.S. Lewis's introduction to the Macmillan edition (1946) notes its accessibility.

entries.label: Ammianus Marcellinus: Res Gestae
entries.hint: The last great Latin historian — a Syrian soldier who continues Tacitus; Books 14–31 survive, covering 353–378, including the Gothic crisis from firsthand experience.
entries.note: Books 14–31 survive (1–13 lost). Oldest MS: Fuldensis (9th c., Fulda). Author identifies as a soldier: 31.16.9. Seyfarth critical edition (1978). Sabbah (1978) on sources.

entries.label: Lactantius: Divine Institutes
entries.loc: Nicomedia; then Trier (tutor to Constantine's son)
entries.hint: The first systematic Latin defence of Christianity as a rational philosophy — Lactantius aims to show educated pagans that Christianity is intellectually coherent.
entries.note: Complete text survives. Earliest MS: 9th c. Brandt-Laubmann critical edition (CSEL 19, 1890). Lactantius was summoned to Trier by Constantine — Jerome, De Viris Ill. 80.

entries.label: Iamblichus: De Mysteriis
entries.hint: Iamblichus's defence of theurgy (ritual magic as philosophy) — the philosophical foundation for Julian's pagan revival and a major text of Neoplatonism.
entries.note: Complete text survives. Earliest MS: 12th c. Des Places critical edition (1966). Julian's debt to Iamblichus: Julian Letter to Iamblichus (spurious but revealing), and Eunapius Lives of the Sophists 457–58.

entries.label: Symmachus: Relatio 3 (Altar of Victory)
entries.hint: Symmachus's elegant plea to Valentinian II to restore the Altar of Victory — the most eloquent surviving defence of Roman paganism and religious pluralism.
entries.note: Text in Seeck's MGH edition (1883). Ambrose's replies (Ep. 17–18) survive alongside. Klein (1972), Symmachus und die heidnisch-christliche Auseinandersetzung. One of the most studied late-antique texts.

entries.label: Jerome begins the Vulgate translation
entries.hint: Pope Damasus commissions Jerome to produce a standard Latin Bible — the Vulgate becomes the authoritative text of Western Christianity for over 1,000 years.
entries.note: Commission by Pope Damasus: Jerome Preface to the Four Gospels (382). Codex Amiatinus (c.692, Biblioteca Medicea Laurenziana, Florence) is the earliest complete copy. Weber-Gryson Stuttgart Vulgate (1969).

entries.label: Constantinian gold standard (solidus)
entries.hint: Constantine introduces the gold solidus at 72 to the pound — it becomes the most stable currency in the world for over 700 years, surviving as the Byzantine nomisma until 1092.
entries.note: Numismatic evidence: RIC VII. First minted c.309 at Trier. Hendy, Studies in the Byzantine Monetary Economy (1985). Stability confirmed by papyrus price data from Egypt (P.Oxy. various).

entries.label: Diocletian's tax reform (iugatio-capitatio)
entries.hint: Diocletian's systematic census-based land and head tax — the most ambitious fiscal reorganisation in Roman history, creating uniform assessable tax units (iuga/capita).
entries.note: Egyptian papyri: P.Cair.Isid. corpus (Karanis village archive, c.296–335). Syrian evidence: inscriptions from Thrace (IGLS). Jones, LRE ch. 13 synthesises the system.

entries.label: Gothic settlement in Moesia (foederati)
entries.hint: After Adrianople, Theodosius settles the Goths inside the empire as a self-governing federate nation — the first time a barbarian people retain their own laws and leaders on Roman soil.
entries.note: Treaty date Oct 382: Themistius Oration 16 (delivered to Theodosius). Zosimus 4.34. Wolfram, History of the Goths (1988). Land allocation terms: Jordanes Getica 27 (later source).

entries.label: Disruption of Eastern trade (Julian's war)
entries.hint: Julian's failed Persian campaign and the cession of Nisibis disrupts the principal eastern trade route; merchants must reroute through Sassanid-controlled territory.
entries.note: Ephrem the Syrian, Carmina Nisibena (eyewitness to the Roman evacuation). Commercial impact inferred from Procopius's later account of treaty terms (Wars 1.2) and decline of Nisibene trade pottery.

entries.label: Constantinople's water infrastructure
entries.hint: Constantinople is built with an entirely new water system from scratch: the Aqueduct of Valens (completed 373), cisterns and harbour infrastructure for a city of 500,000.
entries.note: Aqueduct of Valens: extant (Bozdoğan Kemeri, Istanbul). Completion 373: Socrates HE 4.8. Crow, Bardill & Bayliss, The Water Supply of Byzantine Constantinople (2008).

entries.label: Constantinian frontier fort programme
entries.hint: Constantine rebuilds and extends the Rhine-Danube frontier with a new generation of forts — bridgeheads on the barbarian side of the rivers as aggressive outposts.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus 17.1.11 (Julian rebuilds forts, continuing Constantine's work). Johnson, Late Roman Fortifications (1983). Bridgehead forts at Divitia (Cologne), Deutz: excavated.

entries.label: Gothic alphabet invented (Ulfilas)
entries.hint: Ulfilas creates a new alphabet of 27 letters to transcribe the Gothic language — the first writing system for any Germanic language, invented specifically to translate the Bible.
entries.note: Philostorgius HE 2.5 records Ulfilas as inventor. Codex Argenteus (Uppsala) preserves the result. The alphabet derives from Greek uncials with some Latin and runic elements: Friedrichsen (1926).

entries.label: Church basilica building programme
entries.hint: The legalisation of Christianity triggers the first large-scale public building programme in the Christian world — the Roman basilica form adapted and standardised as the church building.
entries.note: Krautheimer, Early Christian and Byzantine Architecture (1965). Liber Pontificalis records Roman foundations. Eusebius VC 3.29–40 describes the Jerusalem buildings. Engineering continuity from secular basilica documented archaeologically.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Geopolitical shifts, migration & the wider world

entries.label: Sassanid Empire under Shapur II
entries.hint: Shapur II's 70-year reign makes Persia Rome's most dangerous and consistent rival; he besieges Nisibis three times, recaptures it in 363 and kills the emperor Julian.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus (multiple books) is the main Roman source. Tabari's Chronicle (9th c.) for Persian perspective. Shapur II's rock inscriptions at Taq-e Bostan (extant).

entries.label: Hunnic expansion westward
entries.hint: The Huns appear on the Pontic steppe around 370 and push the Goths westward with devastating speed — the chain reaction that produces the Gothic crossing of 376 and Adrianople.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus 31.2–3 (describes Hunnic way of life). Eunapius fragments. Physical evidence: Hunnic cauldron finds cluster west of Don river. Heather, Goths and Romans (1991) ch. 3.

entries.label: Division of empire at Theodosius's death
entries.hint: Theodosius dies leaving the empire to his two sons — 11-year-old Honorius in the West and 18-year-old Arcadius in the East — a division that proves permanent.
entries.note: Death 17 Jan 395: Zosimus 4.59; Claudian, De III Consulatu Honorii. Stilicho's guardianship: Claudian, De Consulatu Stilichonis. Orosius 7.36 for the political consequences.

entries.label: Ammianus on climate and natural disasters
entries.hint: Ammianus Marcellinus records major earthquakes (365 AD tsunami devastating Alexandria), floods and crop failures — unusually rich environmental documentation for the period.
entries.note: Earthquake and tsunami 21 Jul 365: Ammianus 26.10 (eyewitness account of waves reaching Egypt). Kelly Harper, The Fate of Rome (2017) ch. 6. Archaeological evidence: destruction layers at coastal sites.
